sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A social psychology class measured average daily social-media time and loneliness scores in 180 students. The researchers found a positive correlation between the variables, with students who spent more time online tending to report higher loneliness. However, the study did not assign anyone to use social media for a particular amount of time, and it did not measure whether students used online time for passive scrolling or active messaging. The authors discussed multiple possibilities, including reverse causation and third-variable explanations.
questions.stem: What is the safest conclusion from the study as described?

choices.D: Higher social-media time was associated with higher loneliness scores in this sample
choices.A: Social media was proven to cause loneliness
choices.B: Loneliness was proven to cause social-media use
choices.C: No relationship of any kind was observed

explanations.D: Correct. The study found a positive correlation, so the safest statement is an association between more social-media time and higher loneliness. The passage explicitly warns against making a simple causal claim.
explanations.A: Correlation alone cannot prove causation from social media to loneliness.
explanations.B: Reverse causation is possible, but the study does not prove it either.
explanations.C: The passage clearly reports a positive relationship.

questions.stem: Why does the passage mention passive scrolling versus active messaging?

choices.A: Different types of use may represent unmeasured variables that affect interpretation
choices.B: Because the study was actually an experiment
choices.C: Because all online behavior has the same psychological effect
choices.D: Because loneliness can only be measured during messaging

explanations.A: Correct. The authors are signaling that “time online” is a broad category. Different activities could have different effects, so failing to separate them limits how precisely the correlation can be interpreted.
explanations.B: The passage explicitly says the study did not assign usage and therefore was not an experiment.
explanations.C: The authors raise the opposite point: not all online behavior may be equivalent.
explanations.D: Loneliness is not restricted to one type of online activity in the passage.

questions.stem: What is meant by reverse causation in this context?

choices.C: Feeling lonely might lead some students to spend more time on social media
choices.A: Higher loneliness scores make the correlation disappear mathematically
choices.B: Social-media time changes the scoring rubric of the loneliness scale
choices.D: Students become biologically dependent on correlation coefficients

explanations.C: Correct. Reverse causation means the assumed direction of influence may run the other way. Instead of media use causing loneliness, loneliness may increase media use for some students.
explanations.A: Reverse causation is about the direction of influence, not a mathematical deletion of the relationship.
explanations.B: The scoring rubric was not described as changing with media use.
explanations.D: Correlation coefficients are statistics, not substances people become dependent on.

questions.stem: Which additional design would best address the causal ambiguity?

choices.B: A randomized study assigning different patterns of social-media use and following loneliness over time
choices.A: A larger correlational survey with the same measures only
choices.C: An opinion essay about whether social media is good or bad
choices.D: A single interview with the highest-use participant

explanations.B: Correct. Random assignment and prospective follow-up would do the most to isolate whether differences in social-media behavior contribute to later loneliness. The other options would provide less leverage on causality.
explanations.A: A larger survey could improve precision but would still remain correlational.
explanations.C: An opinion essay provides no empirical design improvement.
explanations.D: A single interview might be interesting qualitatively but would not resolve the broader causal question.

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects a third-variable explanation?

choices.A: A factor such as depression or social isolation might increase both media use and loneliness
choices.B: A positive correlation proves there is no third variable
choices.C: The correlation can only be explained by measurement error
choices.D: Third variables are impossible whenever the sample size is above 100

explanations.A: Correct. A third-variable explanation proposes that another factor influences both observed variables, creating or strengthening the correlation. Depression or preexisting social isolation are examples consistent with the passage.
explanations.B: Correlations never rule out third variables on their own.
explanations.C: Measurement error is not the only possible explanation described.
explanations.D: Sample size does not eliminate the possibility of confounding variables.

questions.passage: A public-health methods course evaluated a brief depression screener against a longer clinical interview. Out of 200 students, 50 met the interview criteria for clinically significant depressive symptoms. The brief screener identified 40 of those 50 as positive, but it also flagged 30 students who did not meet interview criteria. Researchers summarized the results using sensitivity and positive predictive value. They discussed how a screening tool can be useful for triage even when it produces some false positives.
questions.stem: According to Figure 1, how many false negatives were there?

explanations.D: Correct. If 50 students truly met interview criteria and 40 of them were correctly identified by the screener, then 10 were missed. Those missed positives are the false negatives.
explanations.A: Thirty is the number of false positives, not false negatives.
explanations.B: Forty is the number of true positives.
explanations.C: One hundred twenty is the number of true negatives.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Confusion-matrix counts comparing a brief depression screener with a longer clinical interview.

questions.stem: What does the screener’s sensitivity represent in this scenario?

choices.C: The proportion of truly positive students who were correctly screened positive
choices.A: The proportion of all screened students who were negative
choices.B: The proportion of positive screens that were false
choices.D: The proportion of truly negative students who were correctly screened negative

explanations.C: Correct. Sensitivity focuses on how well the screener captures actual cases. Here it asks, among the 50 students who truly met interview criteria, what fraction were flagged by the brief screener.
explanations.A: That description does not define sensitivity.
explanations.B: The fraction of positive screens that are false is related to predictive value, not sensitivity.
explanations.D: Correctly identifying true negatives describes specificity, not sensitivity.

questions.stem: Why can a screening tool still be useful even with some false positives?

choices.B: It can efficiently identify people who should receive further evaluation
choices.A: False positives prove the screener has no value
choices.C: A screening tool should replace all clinical interviews entirely
choices.D: False positives mean the screener has perfect specificity

explanations.B: Correct. Screeners are often used for triage rather than final diagnosis. If they catch many true cases, they can still be valuable even when some flagged individuals need later clarification through more detailed assessment.
explanations.A: Many screeners remain useful despite imperfect performance.
explanations.C: The passage explicitly compares the screener with a longer interview, implying follow-up evaluation still matters.
explanations.D: False positives indicate that specificity is less than perfect, not perfect.

questions.stem: What is the positive predictive value of the screener in this sample?

explanations.D: Correct. Positive predictive value asks: among all positive screens, how many were truly positive? There were 40 true positives and 30 false positives, so 70 positive screens total, giving 40/70 ≈ 57%.
explanations.A: Forty out of fifty is the sensitivity, not the positive predictive value.
explanations.B: One hundred twenty out of one hundred fifty describes true negatives among all negatives, which is not PPV.
explanations.C: Thirty out of seventy is the proportion of positive screens that were false, not the proportion that were truly positive.

questions.stem: Which conclusion best matches the passage’s discussion?

choices.A: The brief screener appears useful for triage but should not be treated as equivalent to a full clinical interview
choices.B: The screener is worthless because it is not perfect
choices.C: The screener proves every positive student has depression
choices.D: The screener eliminates the need to think about false negatives

explanations.A: Correct. The passage explicitly frames the tool as useful for triage. That means it has practical value while still requiring awareness of both false positives and missed cases.
explanations.B: Imperfection does not make a screener worthless if it is useful for triage.
explanations.C: Positive screens are not all true cases, as shown by the 30 false positives.
explanations.D: The 10 false negatives show that missed cases still matter.
